state_description: Waiting for human approval of the plan
product_description: Real-time ML recommendation engine with collaborative filtering, contextual bandits for exploration, feature store, A/B testing framework, and sub-100ms serving latency.

plan.description: Define product requirements, success metrics, and technical constraints for the recommendation engine including latency SLAs, throughput targets, and business KPIs.
acceptance_criteria: PRD documents all functional and non-functional requirements
Latency budget allocated across feature retrieval, model inference, and response serialization
Success metrics defined: CTR, NDCG@k, conversion rate, exploration/exploitation ratio
Throughput and concurrency targets documented (e.g. 10k RPS)

plan.description: Design the data model and event schema for user interactions (clicks, impressions, conversions, ratings) and item catalog. Define Kafka topic schemas and Avro/Protobuf contracts.
acceptance_criteria: Protobuf schemas defined and versioned for all event types
Item catalog schema supports sparse and dense feature vectors
User context schema captures real-time signals (device, session, geo)
Schema registry configured with compatibility mode set to BACKWARD

plan.description: Provision core infrastructure: Kubernetes cluster, Kafka cluster for event streaming, Redis cluster for feature caching, and object storage for model artifacts.
acceptance_criteria: Kubernetes cluster running with autoscaling node groups
Kafka cluster deployed with 3 brokers, replication factor 3
Redis cluster running with <1ms p99 read latency under load
S3 bucket with versioning and lifecycle policies for model artifacts
Terraform state stored remotely with locking

plan.description: Design and provision the feature store schema using a dual-store architecture: online store (Redis) for low-latency serving and offline store (PostgreSQL + Parquet on S3) for training.

payload.online_store: Redis Hash — user features, item features, context features
payload.offline_store: PostgreSQL for metadata + Parquet files on S3 for historical features

acceptance_criteria: Redis schema defined with consistent key naming: {entity_type}:{entity_id}:{feature_group}
PostgreSQL schema for feature group registry, entity definitions, and feature metadata
Parquet partition layout defined: /offline/{feature_group}/date={YYYY-MM-DD}/
TTL policies enforced on all Redis keys
Point-in-time correct feature retrieval supported for training data generation

plan.description: Build real-time feature ingestion pipeline: Kafka consumers that compute and write user and item features to the online feature store (Redis) with sub-10ms write latency.
acceptance_criteria: Flink/Kafka Streams jobs process events end-to-end within 10ms (event-time to Redis write)
User click rate, purchase rate, and recency features computed and updated in real time
Item popularity and freshness features updated within 30s of catalog changes
Dead-letter queue configured for malformed events
Consumer lag monitored with alerts at >10k messages

plan.description: Build the offline feature pipeline: batch ETL jobs that generate training datasets from raw interaction logs with point-in-time correct feature joins.
acceptance_criteria: Airflow DAG defined with proper task dependencies and retry logic
Spark job produces training dataset with no data leakage (point-in-time correct joins)
Label generation covers implicit feedback (clicks, purchases) and explicit ratings
Dataset versioned with metadata: row count, date range, feature schema hash
Pipeline completes within 4 hours for 30-day history window

plan.description: Train collaborative filtering model (matrix factorization / neural CF) for candidate generation. Produce user and item embedding vectors stored in the feature store.
acceptance_criteria: Two-Tower model achieves NDCG@10 >= 0.15 on held-out validation set
Recall@50 >= 0.40 (50 candidates contain the positive item 40% of the time)
User and item embeddings exported to FAISS index for ANN retrieval
Model artifacts versioned and stored in S3 with training metadata
Embedding refresh pipeline scheduled weekly
Bias evaluation performed
Model card generated

plan.description: Train contextual bandit ranking model for exploration/exploitation. Implement LinUCB or Neural LinUCB with context features to re-rank candidate items.
acceptance_criteria: Bandit model trains on historical logged bandit data with propensity weighting (IPS estimator)
UCB exploration/exploitation ratio configurable via alpha parameter
Online weight updates processed within 100ms of reward observation
Cumulative regret tracked and decreasing over evaluation window
A/B comparison vs. greedy ranker shows >= 5% CTR lift in offline replay evaluation
Bias evaluation performed
Model card generated

plan.description: Build the FAISS-based approximate nearest neighbor (ANN) index for sub-millisecond candidate retrieval from collaborative filtering embeddings.

payload.index_type: IVFFlat or IVFPQ depending on memory budget

acceptance_criteria: FAISS IVF index built on full item embedding corpus (up to 10M items)
ANN retrieval of top-100 candidates completes within 5ms at 95% recall vs. exact search
Index rebuild pipeline triggered automatically after embedding model retrain
Index hot-swap supported (atomic file replace) with zero serving downtime
Memory footprint documented and within node memory budget
Bias evaluation performed
Model card generated

plan.description: Build the feature store service API: CRUD endpoints for feature group management, bulk feature read/write, and low-latency batch fetch for serving.
acceptance_criteria: Batch feature GET for 100 entities completes within 5ms p99
Redis pipeline batching used (no N+1 round trips)
Feature schema validation on write (type checking, range validation)
OpenAPI spec auto-generated and accurate
gRPC interface available alongside REST for internal service-to-service calls

plan.description: Build the recommendation serving API: the critical latency path orchestrating candidate retrieval, feature fetch, bandit re-ranking, and response assembly in under 100ms.
acceptance_criteria: POST /recommend responds within 100ms p99 under 1000 RPS load
All I/O operations (Redis, FAISS) are fully async (asyncio + aioredis)
Request/response schema validated with Pydantic v2
Trace ID propagated through all downstream calls for distributed tracing
Circuit breakers configured on all external dependencies (Redis, feature store)

plan.description: Build the A/B testing framework: experiment configuration service, user bucketing (consistent hashing), variant assignment, and metric collection infrastructure.

payload.storage: PostgreSQL for experiment config, Redis for active assignments cache

acceptance_criteria: User bucketing is deterministic: same (experiment_id, user_id) always returns same variant
Mutual exclusion between overlapping experiments enforced
Experiment assignment persisted to audit log for reproducibility
Statistical significance calculated on demand with configurable alpha (default 0.05)
Sequential testing (mSPRT) prevents p-hacking from early stopping
Experiment results dashboard queryable via API

plan.description: Configure the agentic coordinator pattern for the recommendation pipeline: a coordinator agent that routes training, evaluation, and serving optimization tasks to specialist sub-agents (data, ML, devops).
acceptance_criteria: SAGE solution YAML files scaffolded for recommendation_engine
Coordinator agent defined in prompts.yaml with routing logic for all specialist agents
Task types registered in tasks.yaml: model_retrain, bandit_update, experiment_analysis, feature_backfill
Agent trigger conditions wired to Kafka events (model_drift topic, data_ready topic)
HITL gate configured for model promotion to production (human approval required)

plan.description: Build the feedback ingestion service: collects real-time reward signals (clicks, purchases, ratings) from clients, updates bandit model weights online, and writes to Kafka for offline training.

payload.deduplication: Redis SETNX with 24h TTL on (user_id, item_id, session_id)

acceptance_criteria: Feedback events published to Kafka within 50ms of receipt
Bandit weight online update triggered within 100ms of reward signal
Duplicate feedback events deduplicated using Redis atomic SET
Feedback linked to recommendation request via trace_id for attribution
Batch feedback endpoint supports up to 1000 events per request

plan.description: Build CI/CD pipelines for model training (MLflow), serving containers (Docker + Kubernetes), and automated canary deployments with latency gate checks.

payload.rollback_trigger: p99 latency > 120ms OR error rate > 0.5% for 5 minutes

acceptance_criteria: MLflow experiment tracking records all training runs with parameters, metrics, and artifacts
Model promotion to staging requires passing offline evaluation gates (NDCG@10, Recall@50)
Canary deployment pipeline deploys to 5% traffic first, monitors for 10 minutes before expanding
Automated rollback triggered if p99 latency exceeds SLA or error rate spikes
All pipeline stages produce auditable logs stored in S3

plan.description: Set up observability stack: distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry + Jaeger), metrics (Prometheus + Grafana), and latency dashboards with SLA alerting.
acceptance_criteria: Every recommendation request has an end-to-end trace spanning all services
Latency breakdown visible per stage: feature_fetch, ann_retrieval, ranking, serialization
Grafana dashboard shows real-time p50/p95/p99 latency with SLA line at 100ms
PagerDuty or Slack alert fires within 2 minutes of SLA breach
Feature store cache hit rate monitored (target > 98%)

plan.description: Perform threat model and security review for the recommendation system: user data access controls, feature store authorization, experiment data integrity, and PII handling.

pii_fields: no raw email/name in feature store
data_governance: user data deletion cascades to feature store and offline parquet

acceptance_criteria: Threat model document covers STRIDE categories for all API surfaces
PII fields pseudonymized before storage (user_id is a UUID, no raw identifiers)
RBAC enforced: read-only role for analytics, write role for ingestion services only
GDPR deletion workflow documented: wipes Redis keys, marks Parquet records as deleted
Dependency SBOM generated and scanned for CVEs with no critical findings unmitigated

plan.description: Write unit and integration tests for all backend services: feature store service, recommendation API, A/B testing framework, and feedback ingestion.

integration: end-to-end recommend flow with mocked Redis and FAISS

acceptance_criteria: Unit test coverage >= 85% for src/ modules
Integration tests cover happy path and failure modes (Redis down, FAISS miss, empty candidates)
Bandit UCB arithmetic verified against known numerical examples
A/B bucketing test: 10k simulated users produce statistically equal split (chi-square test passes)
All tests run in CI in under 5 minutes without external dependencies

plan.description: End-to-end system test and load test: validate sub-100ms p99 latency under realistic production load (1000+ RPS) and verify correctness of the full recommendation pipeline.

correctness_checks: Recommended items are from active catalog only
Experiment variant assignment is stable across requests
Feedback loop updates bandit weights within 100ms

acceptance_criteria: 1000 RPS sustained for 10 minutes with p99 latency < 100ms
Zero correctness violations: no inactive items, no cross-experiment contamination
System recovers from Redis node failure within 30 seconds with no serving errors
Cold start (empty cache) degrades gracefully: latency increases but requests succeed
Load test report generated with percentile breakdown and error rate

plan.description: Build the recommendation engine admin dashboard: experiment management UI, model performance monitoring, feature store explorer, and A/B test results visualization.

pages: ModelMetricsPage — NDCG, Recall, reward rate over time
FeatureStorePage — inspect feature groups, entity features

acceptance_criteria: Experiments page supports full CRUD: create experiment, set traffic split, view assignments, conclude
Model metrics page shows NDCG@10 and Recall@50 trend lines with training run annotations
Feature store page allows searching entities by ID and inspecting feature values with TTL
Latency dashboard shows real-time p50/p95/p99 with SLA threshold line
All pages load within 2 seconds and are mobile-responsive

plan.description: Build the model evaluation and drift detection pipeline: offline evaluation harness, online A/B metric collection, and automated drift alerts triggering retraining.

payload.offline_eval: Replay evaluation on held-out log data with IPS correction
payload.online_eval: Live experiment metrics from A/B framework
payload.retraining_trigger: automated Airflow DAG trigger on drift detection
payload.eval_cadence: daily offline eval + continuous online monitoring

acceptance_criteria: Offline replay evaluation runs daily and posts results to MLflow
PSI computed for top-20 input features daily; alert fires if PSI > 0.2
Drift detection triggers automatic retraining DAG with Slack notification
Model promoted to production only if new model outperforms current on offline eval
Evaluation results retained for 90 days for audit trail
Bias evaluation performed
Model card generated

plan.description: Write technical documentation: system architecture overview, API reference, runbook for on-call, and developer onboarding guide.

docs: architecture.md — system diagram, data flow, latency budget breakdown
api_reference.md — all endpoints, request/response schemas, error codes
oncall_runbook.md — alerts, triage steps, rollback procedures
developer_guide.md — local setup, adding features, running tests
ab_testing_guide.md — how to design and run experiments

acceptance_criteria: Architecture doc includes sequence diagram for the full recommend request flow
API reference auto-generated from OpenAPI spec and manually reviewed for accuracy
On-call runbook covers every Prometheus alert with triage steps and escalation path
Developer can set up local environment and run all tests following the guide with zero tribal knowledge
A/B testing guide explains statistical methodology and early-stopping policy

flaws: Step 5 claims Flink end-to-end latency of 10ms (event-time to Redis write). Flink's minimum checkpoint interval is typically 100ms–1s, and micro-batch processing adds inherent buffering. 10ms is achievable only with Kafka Streams in per-record mode with zero windowing — the plan conflates processing-time latency with event-time semantics and will fail this acceptance criterion in production.

Step 8 requires Neural LinUCB online weight updates within 100ms. At 10k RPS with even 1% CTR, that is 100 reward signals/second each triggering a matrix update (Sherman-Morrison or gradient step). The plan proposes a 'Vowpal Wabbit + custom PyTorch wrapper' — these are fundamentally incompatible paradigms (VW is online-learning native; PyTorch requires explicit gradient management). The wrapper will be a correctness and maintenance liability.

Step 9 describes FAISS hot-swap as 'atomic file replace with zero serving downtime.' A file-system atomic replace does not produce an atomic in-memory swap. Each serving pod holds the index in RAM; replacing the file on disk requires explicit reload logic per pod. Under rolling reloads, pods serve from different index versions simultaneously with no version-skew handling described.

Step 11's latency budget sums to 40ms best-case (2+5+5+10+15+3). This leaves only 60ms of headroom for p99 at 100ms SLA. It does not account for: Redis cluster network jitter (can spike 20–30ms at p99 under GC pauses), TLS overhead on Redis connections, Python async event loop scheduling overhead, or serialization cost for 100-item feature payloads. The p99 SLA will be violated under realistic production conditions without explicit tail-latency mitigation.

Step 8's IPS (Inverse Propensity Score) estimator for offline bandit evaluation requires propensity scores — the probability that each item was shown under the logging policy. Neither Step 11 (serving) nor Step 14 (feedback ingestion) includes propensity logging in the request/response schema. Without stored propensities, the IPS estimator is mathematically invalid and will produce biased offline evaluation results.

Step 4 claims 'point-in-time correct feature retrieval' using Redis as the online store with TTL eviction. Redis TTLs will evict features that existed at prediction time before the training pipeline runs. There is no mechanism to snapshot feature values at request time for later training label joins. This creates training/serving skew — a fundamental ML correctness failure that will silently degrade model quality.

Step 12 claims mutual exclusion between overlapping experiments is 'enforced' but provides no mechanism. SHA-256 consistent hashing assigns users to buckets deterministically but does not prevent the same user from being in two concurrent experiments simultaneously. Mutual exclusion requires an explicit namespace/layer isolation system (as used by Google/Meta). Without this, experiment contamination will corrupt statistical results.

Step 14 triggers bandit online weight updates within 100ms of reward arrival. Under concurrency, multiple feedback events for the same bandit model arrive simultaneously. Neural LinUCB weight matrices are not thread-safe for concurrent updates. The plan does not address locking strategy, update queuing, or eventual consistency semantics — race conditions will corrupt model weights silently.

The cold start problem is unaddressed. New users (no interaction history) and new items (no embeddings) are mentioned only as a chaos scenario in Step 19. At production scale, cold-start requests constitute a significant fraction of traffic and require fallback strategies (popularity-based, content-based, or exploration-heavy bandits). The Two-Tower model produces no embedding for unseen entities.

FAISS is deployed 'in-process within the serving container' (Step 9) but the serving API scales horizontally on Kubernetes (Step 11). Each pod holds its own FAISS index in RAM. Index rebuilds (Step 9) must propagate atomically to all N pods simultaneously. The plan provides no pod coordination mechanism — pods will serve from stale indices of different ages during rolling updates, breaking recall guarantees.

Step 17's GDPR deletion requirement says 'marks Parquet records as deleted.' Parquet is an immutable columnar format — records cannot be deleted in place. Actual deletion requires rewriting affected partitions. At scale (30-day Parquet history across multiple feature groups), this is a multi-hour Spark job per deletion request. The plan underestimates this by an order of magnitude.
suggestions: Replace Flink with Kafka Streams for the real-time feature pipeline (Step 5). Kafka Streams processes records synchronously without checkpointing overhead and can achieve sub-10ms Redis writes. Reserve Flink only if complex windowed aggregations are required.

Decouple Neural LinUCB online updates from the feedback ingestion hot path (Step 8/14). Write rewards to a bounded in-memory queue and process updates asynchronously in a single-writer thread. Expose a 'reward freshness' metric rather than promising 100ms synchronous update — this is the correct production architecture for online bandits.

Add propensity logging to Step 11 immediately. The serving response should include `{item_id, score, position, propensity}` tuples. Propensities must be persisted alongside feedback events (Step 14) for IPS correctness. This is a schema change that is expensive to retrofit after deployment.

Implement feature snapshots for training/serving alignment: when a recommendation request is served, write a snapshot of the feature values used (keyed by trace_id) to a time-limited store (Redis with 7-day TTL). The offline pipeline joins training labels to these snapshots instead of the live feature store, eliminating training/serving skew.

Add a model serving sidecar pattern for FAISS: instead of in-process per pod, deploy a shared FAISS service (or use Redis Stack with RediSearch for ANN) that all serving pods query. This eliminates the pod coordination problem for index updates and centralizes memory usage.

For A/B mutual exclusion (Step 12), implement an experiment namespace/layer system: each experiment is assigned to an orthogonal dimension, and user assignment is computed as hash(user_id + layer_salt). Users can participate in one experiment per layer simultaneously. Document this in the PRD as a first-class design constraint.

Add explicit Redis memory sizing to Step 3/4. At 10M items with 256-dim float32 embeddings: 10M × 256 × 4B = 10GB for item embeddings alone, before user features or interaction data. A 6-node cluster with 16GB/node gives 96GB total — model this explicitly against your feature groups to validate the cluster is sized correctly.

Replace 'atomic file replace' in Step 9 with a versioned index endpoint: the serving container polls a model registry (MLflow) for the latest index version and loads it into a shadow slot, then atomically swaps the pointer. Use a read-write lock per pod to prevent serving during swap. Coordinate across pods via a shared 'active_index_version' key in Redis.

For GDPR deletion (Step 17), implement a deletion ledger pattern: maintain a `deleted_users` table in PostgreSQL. All Spark training jobs filter against this table at read time. Schedule quarterly full Parquet rewrites to physically remove deleted records. This is the only approach that scales without per-deletion Spark jobs.

Add explicit rate limiting (token bucket or leaky bucket) to the serving API (Step 11) with per-user and per-client-id limits. Without this, a single misbehaving client can saturate Redis and blow the p99 SLA for all users.
missing: Propensity logging schema and storage strategy — required for IPS bandit evaluation in Step 8 to be mathematically valid
Cold start strategy for new users and new items — a critical production gap affecting a significant fraction of traffic
Feature snapshot mechanism for training/serving alignment — absence guarantees training data bias
FAISS index distribution and synchronization strategy across horizontal pod replicas
Redis memory budget and eviction policy selection (allkeys-lru vs. volatile-lru) — critical for feature store TTL behavior under memory pressure
Canary metric isolation strategy in Step 15 — how are 5% canary request metrics separated from 95% control in Prometheus/Grafana for valid comparison
Bandit exploration/exploitation policy for cold-start items with no reward history
Network topology and service mesh configuration (how do serving pods discover Redis, feature store, FAISS service)
Kafka topic ACLs and broker authentication — no mention of mTLS or SASL between producers and consumers
Model rollback strategy beyond binary 'rollback on SLA breach' — what happens to bandit weights that were updated online during a bad model's serving window
Data retention and S3 cost model for Parquet offline store — 30-day history with daily partitions across multiple feature groups at scale needs explicit sizing
Experiment sample size and power calculation tooling in Step 12 — without minimum detectable effect and required sample size pre-calculation, experiments will be underpowered or run too long
security_risks: Redis cluster has no mention of TLS in-transit or Redis AUTH. Feature store data contains behavioral signals tied to pseudonymized user IDs. Unencrypted Redis in a multi-tenant Kubernetes cluster is a data exposure risk if network policies are misconfigured.

Kafka topics have no ACL specification. Any service that can reach the Kafka brokers can read `raw_feedback` or `bandit_rewards` topics, or inject malicious events that corrupt bandit model weights (model poisoning via feedback injection).

FAISS index artifacts are stored in S3 with no integrity verification mentioned. A compromised S3 bucket or misconfigured IAM role allows injection of a poisoned FAISS index, causing the recommendation engine to serve attacker-controlled items to all users.

JWT validation in Step 17 is described at the API level but the feature store service (Step 10) handles internal service-to-service calls. If the feature store trusts internal network calls without verifying service identity (mTLS or service account tokens), a compromised pod can exfiltrate all user features.

A/B experiment assignment logs contain the full mapping of user_id to experiment variant. If these are stored in plaintext in the audit log alongside behavioral signals, they constitute a behavioral profile that is sensitive under GDPR and could be used to re-identify pseudonymized users.

The feedback deduplication mechanism (Step 14) uses Redis SETNX on (user_id, item_id, session_id). An adversary who can predict or enumerate session_ids can replay feedback events after the 24h TTL expires, polluting the bandit reward signal without triggering deduplication.
final_review.summary: This is a technically sophisticated and architecturally sound plan that demonstrates genuine ML systems expertise — the component selection (Two-Tower + LinUCB + FAISS + dual-store feature store) is appropriate, the latency budget decomposition is the right approach, and the CI/CD canary strategy is production-grade. However, the plan scores 63 because it contains several critical correctness gaps that would cause silent failures in production rather than visible crashes: the absence of propensity logging makes the bandit's offline evaluation mathematically invalid; the missing feature snapshot mechanism guarantees training/serving skew that will degrade model quality over time; the FAISS in-process deployment pattern breaks under horizontal scaling without a coordination mechanism; and the GDPR Parquet deletion claim is not implementable as described. The 100ms online bandit update promise under concurrent load is an unverified performance claim that will likely require fundamental architectural change (async update queue) rather than optimization. None of these are fatal to the overall design — they are tractable engineering problems — but they need explicit resolution before this plan can be executed against the stated acceptance criteria. Recommend addressing the propensity logging, feature snapshot, FAISS pod coordination, and bandit concurrency gaps before committing to the current acceptance criteria.

JWT validation in Step 17 is described at the API level but the feature store service (Step 10) handles internal service-to-service calls. If the feature store trusts internal network calls without verifying service identity (mTLS or service account tokens), a compromised pod can exfiltrate all user features.

A/B experiment assignment logs contain the full mapping of user_id to experiment variant. If these are stored in plaintext in the audit log alongside behavioral signals, they constitute a behavioral profile that is sensitive under GDPR and could be used to re-identify pseudonymized users.

The feedback deduplication mechanism (Step 14) uses Redis SETNX on (user_id, item_id, session_id). An adversary who can predict or enumerate session_ids can replay feedback events after the 24h TTL expires, polluting the bandit reward signal without triggering deduplication.
history.summary: This is a technically sophisticated and architecturally sound plan that demonstrates genuine ML systems expertise — the component selection (Two-Tower + LinUCB + FAISS + dual-store feature store) is appropriate, the latency budget decomposition is the right approach, and the CI/CD canary strategy is production-grade. However, the plan scores 63 because it contains several critical correctness gaps that would cause silent failures in production rather than visible crashes: the absence of propensity logging makes the bandit's offline evaluation mathematically invalid; the missing feature snapshot mechanism guarantees training/serving skew that will degrade model quality over time; the FAISS in-process deployment pattern breaks under horizontal scaling without a coordination mechanism; and the GDPR Parquet deletion claim is not implementable as described. The 100ms online bandit update promise under concurrent load is an unverified performance claim that will likely require fundamental architectural change (async update queue) rather than optimization. None of these are fatal to the overall design — they are tractable engineering problems — but they need explicit resolution before this plan can be executed against the stated acceptance criteria. Recommend addressing the propensity logging, feature snapshot, FAISS pod coordination, and bandit concurrency gaps before committing to the current acceptance criteria.
